core_behavior: The system must enforce a rate limit of 100 requests per minute on a designated API endpoint, scoped to each individual authenticated user.
Each request from an authenticated user is counted against that user's quota.

When a user exceeds 100 requests within the current rate limit window, all subsequent requests within that window must be rejected with an HTTP 429 Too Many Requests response that includes a Retry-After header indicating when the user may retry.
The rate limiting mechanism must operate using a fixed window counter strategy.
The window is 60 seconds in duration.
The counter resets at the start of each new window.
inputs: User identity token from HTTP request header (Authorization: Bearer <token>), used to identify and authenticate the user.
Request timestamp from server clock (UTC), used to determine the current window.
Target endpoint path from HTTP request URI, used to scope rate limiting to the correct endpoint.
HTTP method from HTTP request method, included in endpoint matching.
RATE_LIMIT_MAX_REQUESTS: 100 — Maximum allowed requests per window.
RATE_LIMIT_WINDOW_SECONDS: 60 — Duration of the rate limit window in seconds.
RATE_LIMIT_SCOPE: Per authenticated user — Isolation unit for counters.
outputs: When request is allowed (counter ≤ 100), the request proceeds normally.
X-RateLimit-Limit header with value 100 must be appended to every response from the rate-limited endpoint, regardless of whether the limit has been reached.
X-RateLimit-Remaining header with value 100 - current_count must be appended to every response from the rate-limited endpoint.
X-RateLimit-Reset header with Unix timestamp (UTC) indicating the time at which the current window expires and the counter resets must be appended to every response from the rate-limited endpoint.
When request is rejected (counter > 100), the system returns an HTTP 429 Too Many Requests response.
Retry-After header with integer value in seconds until the current window resets, calculated as window_end_timestamp - current_timestamp, rounded up to the nearest whole second, minimum value of 1.
X-RateLimit-Limit header with value 100 on rejected responses.
X-RateLimit-Remaining header with value 0 on rejected responses.
X-RateLimit-Reset header with Unix timestamp (UTC) of window expiry on rejected responses.
Content-Type header with value application/json on rejected responses.

Response body JSON with fields: error set to 'rate_limit_exceeded', message set to 'You have exceeded the rate limit of 100 requests per minute. Please retry after {N} seconds.', and retry_after_seconds set to N, where N matches the value of the Retry-After header.
assumptions: A fixed window (tumbling window) algorithm is used rather than a sliding window or token bucket.
The window aligns to clock-minute boundaries (e.g., 14:03:00–14:03:59 UTC) rather than being relative to the first request.
The fixed window strategy permits a burst of up to 200 requests across a window boundary; this trade-off is accepted unless the requirement is revised.
Rate limiting is applied only to authenticated users.
Unauthenticated requests (missing, invalid, or expired tokens) must be rejected with 401 Unauthorized before the rate limit check is performed.
Unauthenticated requests do not consume rate limit quota.
failure_modes: When a user exceeds 100 requests within the current rate limit window, all subsequent requests within that window must be rejected with an HTTP 429 Too Many Requests response.
Unauthenticated requests (missing, invalid, or expired tokens) must be rejected with 401 Unauthorized before the rate limit check is performed.
edge_cases: The fixed window strategy permits a burst of up to 200 requests across a window boundary.
Retry-After value has a minimum value of 1 second.
X-RateLimit-Remaining is 0 when the limit has been exceeded.
security_constraints: Rate limiting is applied only to authenticated users.
Unauthenticated requests (missing, invalid, or expired tokens) must be rejected with 401 Unauthorized before the rate limit check is performed.
Unauthenticated requests do not consume rate limit quota.
